segments.title: Why proximity to events shapes source ranking
segments.summary: Historians prioritize primary sources and rank evidence by how closely it connects to the events being studied.
segments.body: Historians rank sources by how far they are removed from the event under study. An eyewitness account or an official document compiled at the time by people involved can count as primary evidence. Its proximity makes it especially valuable for recovering what participants knew or remembered, although it still has to be compared critically with other accounts. The labels describe a source's relationship to events rather than guaranteeing that any single version is the whole truth.
segments.question: Why do historians prioritize primary sources and rank evidence as primary, secondary, or tertiary?
segments.answerShort: Because the closer a source is to the event, the more directly it can preserve what participants saw, recorded, or remembered.

evidence.note: The transcript defines source ranks by their removal from an event and describes contemporary eyewitness accounts and official documents as primary.

segments.title: Jutland reports show why versions matter
segments.summary: The Jutland example shows how an original account, an official published report, and a later book can preserve competing versions of the same event.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses the reporting around Jutland to show how a secondary compilation can reshape primary information. Original material may say one thing while the version issued as an official report says another. The complication does not end there, because an officer whose report was altered or suppressed may publish the original account separately. Comparing versions can therefore reveal disagreement, institutional pressure, and the personal stakes behind a supposedly settled record.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts original Jutland information with a published report and describes an officer publishing an earlier report after it was blocked.

segments.title: Reconstructing a battle from different viewpoints
segments.summary: Tertiary synthesis depends on comparing firsthand perspectives because an engine-room sailor and a gun-director crew experience the same battle differently.
segments.body: The closing explanation first defines tertiary work as what historians build from the earlier layers of information. It then shifts from labels to historical method. A person in a ship's engine room and someone operating the gun director occupy different parts of the same battle, so each notices different evidence and reaches different conclusions. Historians need to gather those perspectives and place them in context rather than treating one account as the whole story. Source ranking shows how close the available evidence comes to events, while corroboration helps build the fullest possible reconstruction.

evidence.note: The transcript defines tertiary work, then explains that historians compare contrasting perspectives such as those from an engine room and a gun director.
